topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The attic air hung thick and dusty, smelling of aged paper and forgotten lavender. I was supposed to be clearing out old photo albums, but my fingers brushed against a large, unmarked cardboard box tucked behind a stack of mildewed textbooks. Curiosity, a sudden, unwelcome guest, pulled me toward it. Inside, nestled amongst brittle photographs, was a meticulously organized collection of every school assignment I had ever turned in, from kindergarten essays to final thesis drafts. A cold knot tightened in my stomach. I pulled out a crumpled history report, the ink still sharp, and stared at the neat handwriting. My jaw tightened. How could she keep all of this? It felt like a deliberate, almost mocking gesture. I slammed the box shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet space.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" I muttered, the word tasting bitter on my tongue. I found the evidence tucked inside an old shoebox—a binder, spine cracked, filled spine-to-spine with every piece of academic work I’d ever produced. My breath hitched. It wasn't just the sheer volume; it was the careful cataloging. My hands began to tremble as I ran a finger over a meticulously labeled folder marked 'Algebra Midterm, 10th Grade.' A flush crept up my neck. This wasn't helpful; it was an overreach, an unnecessary hoarding of my effort. I stood up abruptly, pacing the cramped room. I needed an explanation, a justification for this bizarre, overwhelming act of preservation.

[story 3]

The sheer audacity of it made my chest feel tight. I found the stash hidden beneath a pile of winter blankets in the seldom-used storage closet. It was an entire archive of my scholastic endeavors. I picked up a particularly embarrassing science project, the glitter glue still faintly visible on the cardboard. A low, frustrated sound escaped my throat. I felt a distinct heat rising in my cheeks, a burning sensation that had nothing to do with the temperature. I stared at the collection, feeling a profound sense of being undermined. This wasn't just tidiness; it was an assertion of ownership over my work that felt entirely misplaced and deeply disrespectful. I dropped the papers back down, refusing to look at them again.
